refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to eliminate central points of failure and enable partial operation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Combine redundancy (DP1), modularity (DP2), and monitoring (DP3, DP6) in an integrated availability architecture with defined recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Integrated availability architecture combining (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) modular hot-swappable design for rapid component replacement, (3) predictive condition monitoring to detect degradation early, and (4) comprehensive fault detection and isolation with automated diagnostics—all coordinated to achieve target availability ≥0.97 by minimizing both failure frequency and mean-time-to-repair.

potential_sources: Redundancy strategy (DP1 component) must be coordinated with failover logic timing to avoid race conditions or cascading failures during switchover.
Modular design (DP2 component) requires standardized interfaces and state synchronization protocols to ensure hot-swap compatibility without data loss.
Predictive maintenance (DP3 component) depends on sensor data quality and model accuracy; poor condition monitoring can mask imminent failures.
FDIA system (DP6 component) must integrate with failover triggers to avoid conflicting recovery actions.
Distributed control (DP5 concept, if adopted) introduces synchronization overhead and potential split-brain scenarios; must be carefully scoped.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 is a composite design that integrates multiple independent strategies. Coupling is manageable if each sub-strategy (redundancy, modularity, monitoring, diagnostics) is assigned clear ownership boundaries (IM layer) and interfaces are explicitly defined. Risk is moderate if integration testing and failure mode analysis are rigorous.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target availability ≥0.97 is achievable with the proposed architecture given worksite constraints (e.g., environmental conditions, power supply stability, maintenance access).
ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be procured, integrated, and maintained within budget and schedule constraints.
ASSUMPTION: Predictive maintenance models can be calibrated with sufficient historical data or validated through accelerated life testing.
RISK: If redundancy is passive (standby) rather than active, failover latency may cause brief unavailability; active redundancy increases complexity and cost.
RISK: Modular design may introduce interface overhead (communication latency, synchronization delays) that degrades performance or availability if not carefully engineered.
RISK: Condition monitoring sensors may themselves fail or provide false positives/negatives, reducing diagnostic confidence and increasing unnecessary maintenance actions.
RISK: Distributed control without strong synchronization guarantees may lead to inconsistent state and data corruption during partial failures.
RISK: MTTR depends on spare parts availability, technician training, and accessibility of failed components; if not managed, MTTR can exceed recovery time objectives.
RISK: Evaluation period duration and worksite operational profile (duty cycle, environmental stress) must be representative; short or atypical evaluation may not validate sustained availability.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP7 by explicitly decomposing it into four sub-DPs with clear ownership and interface contracts: (1) DP7a: Redundancy & Failover Strategy (defines which subsystems are redundant, failover triggers, and switchover timing); (2) DP7b: Modular Component Design (specifies hot-swap interface standards, state synchronization protocol, and compatibility matrix); (3) DP7c: Condition Monitoring & Predictive Maintenance (defines sensor suite, data collection frequency, degradation models, and alert thresholds); (4) DP7d: Fault Detection, Isolation & Diagnostics (specifies diagnostic algorithms, alert routing, and operator interface). Each sub-DP should map to a distinct IM (implementation module/ownership boundary) to enable independent verification and reduce integration risk. Additionally, establish explicit acceptance criteria for each sub-DP (e.g., failover latency <5 seconds, MTTR <2 hours, sensor false-positive rate <5%) and define the evaluation protocol (duration, duty cycle, failure injection scenarios) to validate that the integrated design achieves ≥0.97 availability under realistic worksite conditions.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated availability architecture encompassing redundancy coordination, hot-swap capability management, predictive condition monitoring orchestration, and fault detection/isolation/diagnostics across all subsystems within the networked ground handling ecosystem.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound sensor data, subsystem health metrics, and operational state from Vehicle and Teleoperation for condition monitoring and fault detection.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound commands to Vehicle and Teleoperation to trigger failover actions, hot-swap procedures, and diagnostic routines.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound critical alerts to Safety Services when availability thresholds are at risk or fault isolation requires safety intervention.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication for redundancy coordination, inter-subsystem heartbeats, and distributed diagnostics across the NGHE ecosystem.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the system-level integrator responsible for coordinating availability across all subsystems. Redundancy logic, failover orchestration, hot-swap management, predictive monitoring, and fault diagnostics are all system-wide concerns that naturally belong at this level. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement local health reporting and respond to failover/swap commands, but core availability architecture resides here.

Poor containment: Vehicle is a single subsystem; DP7 requires coordination across multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). Redundancy, failover, and diagnostics cannot be localized to Vehicle alone. High diffusion: availability logic would scatter across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution, violating cohesion.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a single subsystem; DP7 is a system-wide availability strategy. Predictive monitoring and fault isolation require visibility across all subsystems. High diffusion: availability coordination would be fragmented across multiple modules.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a specialized safety-critical module, not a system integrator. DP7 includes operational availability logic (redundancy, hot-swap, predictive monitoring) that is distinct from safety assurance. High diffusion: mixing operational availability with safety services creates coupling and violates separation of concerns. Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert outputs from NGHE Solution, not own the availability architecture.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP7 is fundamentally a system-level availability orchestration strategy that coordinates redundancy, failover, condition monitoring, and diagnostics across all subsystems. NGHE Solution, as the top-level system integrator, is the natural and most cohesive boundary for this DP. Vehicle and Teleoperation will implement local health reporting and respond to commands; Safety Services will consume safety-critical alerts. The architecture avoids diffusion by centralizing availability logic at the system level while maintaining clear, narrow interfaces for subsystem interaction.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic 'god module' without internal decomposition, future maintenance and testing of availability sub-functions (redundancy vs. diagnostics vs. predictive monitoring) may suffer. Mitigation: Decompose DP7 into child FRs (e.g., FR7.1 for redundancy coordination, FR7.2 for predictive monitoring, FR7.3 for fault isolation) and map each to a cohesive internal DP and sub-module within NGHE Solution.

Risk: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement health-reporting and failover-response logic; if their interfaces are not well-defined, availability coordination may become implicit and fragile. Mitigation: Formalize Telemetry and Tasking interface contracts (schema, timing, error handling) to ensure Vehicle and Teleoperation can reliably participate in the availability architecture.

Risk: Predictive condition monitoring may require machine learning or statistical models that are computationally intensive; if NGHE Solution is resource-constrained, monitoring latency could degrade availability. Mitigation: Evaluate whether predictive monitoring should be a separate, dedicated module (e.g., 'Condition Monitoring Service') with a clear interface to NGHE Solution, or if it can be efficiently embedded within NGHE Solution's core logic.

Risk: Network Link reliability is critical for redundancy coordination and distributed diagnostics; if Network Link fails, availability architecture may collapse. Mitigation: Ensure redundancy logic includes graceful degradation when Network Link is unavailable, and design failover to not depend on real-time network communication.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 directly and comprehensively addresses FR (operational availability ≥0.97). The four pillars of DP7 (redundancy with automatic failover, modular hot-swappable design, predictive condition monitoring, fault detection/isolation/diagnostics) are logically sound and collectively sufficient to minimize both failure frequency and mean-time-to-repair. The DP is expressed at appropriate abstraction level and includes quantified target (≥0.97). No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: CRITICAL: Predictive condition monitoring sub-function may require dedicated computational resources (ML/statistical models). If embedded monolithically in NGHE Solution without clear internal module boundary, monitoring latency could degrade availability performance. Recommend explicit decomposition into FR7.3 (Predictive Condition Monitoring) with dedicated IM (e.g., 'Condition Monitoring Service') or clearly scoped internal sub-module within NGHE Solution.

HIGH: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems must implement local health-reporting and failover-response logic as participants in the availability architecture. If Telemetry and Tasking interface contracts are not formally specified (schema, timing guarantees, error handling, retry semantics), availability coordination becomes implicit and fragile. Recommend formalized interface specifications before detailed design.

MEDIUM: Network Link is a critical dependency for redundancy coordination and distributed diagnostics. If Network Link fails, availability architecture may degrade or collapse. Current DP does not explicitly address graceful degradation when Network Link is unavailable. Recommend explicit sub-requirement (FR7.4) for 'Availability under Network Partition' with corresponding DP addressing local failover without real-time network communication.

MEDIUM: NGHE Solution containment score of 0.92 is strong, but residual 0.08 diffusion indicates Vehicle and Teleoperation carry distributed health-reporting and response responsibilities. Ensure these responsibilities are minimal, well-defined, and do not leak availability orchestration logic into subsystems. Recommend interface-driven design to enforce separation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: DP7 is correctly assigned to NGHE Solution as the system-level availability orchestrator. This placement avoids diffusion across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, each of which would create coupling and violate cohesion if assigned ownership. NGHE Solution's role as integrator is appropriate. However, internal decomposition of DP7 into child DPs (redundancy coordination, predictive monitoring, fault isolation, diagnostics) is essential to prevent monolithic bloat and to enable independent testing and evolution of availability sub-functions. Recommend hierarchical decomposition: FR7 → {FR7.1, FR7.2, FR7.3, FR7.4} with corresponding DPs and internal IMs (e.g., 'Redundancy Coordinator', 'Condition Monitor', 'Fault Isolator', 'Diagnostics Engine') within NGHE Solution boundary.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FEASIBLE with caveats. Acceptance criterion (measured availability ≥0.97 over agreed evaluation period) is quantified and measurable. However, verification requires: (1) instrumentation of all subsystems to collect telemetry and health metrics; (2) controlled failure injection or field observation over sufficient duration to validate availability target; (3) clear definition of 'evaluation period' (e.g., 30 days, 90 days) and failure modes included in availability calculation (e.g., does planned maintenance count as downtime?). Recommend explicit test plan and acceptance test procedure before gate closure.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. FR is allocated to NGHE Solution IM with well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert out, Network Link bidirectional). Subsystem responsibilities are implicit but not formally allocated: Vehicle and Teleoperation must report health and respond to commands, but no explicit FR is assigned to them for availability participation. Recommend creating child FRs for subsystem-level availability requirements (e.g., FR7.1.1: 'Vehicle shall report health status every 100ms with <50ms latency') to clarify allocation and enable independent verification.

recommended_revisions: DECOMPOSE DP7 into hierarchical child DPs: FR7.1 (Redundancy Coordination), FR7.2 (Hot-Swap Management), FR7.3 (Predictive Condition Monitoring), FR7.4 (Fault Detection, Isolation, Diagnostics), FR7.5 (Availability under Network Partition). Map each to a cohesive internal DP and sub-module within NGHE Solution to prevent monolithic bloat and enable independent testing.

FORMALIZE Telemetry and Tasking interface contracts: Define schema (e.g., JSON/protobuf), timing guarantees (e.g., max latency, heartbeat interval), error handling (e.g., timeout, retry, fallback), and validation rules. Ensure Vehicle and Teleoperation can reliably participate in availability architecture without implicit coupling.

EVALUATE Predictive Condition Monitoring placement: Determine whether predictive monitoring (ML/statistical models) should be a dedicated, separately-owned module (e.g., 'Condition Monitoring Service') with clear interface to NGHE Solution, or efficiently embedded within NGHE Solution's core logic. If separate, define ownership, resource allocation, and latency SLA.

ADD explicit sub-requirement for graceful degradation: Create FR7.5 (Availability under Network Partition) with DP addressing local failover and diagnostics when Network Link is unavailable. Ensure redundancy logic does not depend on real-time network communication for critical failover decisions.

CREATE subsystem-level availability FRs: Allocate explicit child FRs to Vehicle and Teleoperation (e.g., FR7.1.1: 'Vehicle shall report health status with <50ms latency', FR7.2.1: 'Teleoperation shall respond to failover commands within 200ms'). Enable independent verification and clarify subsystem responsibilities.

DEFINE evaluation period and failure modes: Specify duration of availability measurement (e.g., 30 days, 90 days), failure modes included in availability calculation (e.g., planned maintenance, network outages, subsystem faults), and acceptance test procedure. Ensure acceptance criterion is unambiguous and verifiable.

DOCUMENT safety boundary: Clarify relationship between DP7 (operational availability) and Safety Services (safety assurance). Ensure SafetyAlert interface is the only coupling point and that availability logic does not leak into safety-critical decisions. Recommend separate safety case for availability-related hazards.

decision.justification: Gate results show FR7 and DP7 pass logical and allocation checks, but gate output explicitly identifies critical diffusion risks and monolithic bloat hazards that demand hierarchical decomposition before detailed design. The recommended_revisions list seven actionable decomposition targets, with CRITICAL and HIGH severity items (predictive monitoring resource isolation, interface formalization, network partition handling) that cannot be deferred. Safety criticality amplifies the need for explicit sub-requirement allocation to subsystems and clear module boundaries. Decomposition is not optional refinement—it is mandatory risk mitigation to prevent implicit coupling, latency degradation, and verification gaps in a safety-critical availability architecture.
next_level_sub_frs: FR7.1: Coordinate redundancy activation and failover across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to ensure seamless transition without service interruption.
FR7.2: Manage hot-swappable module insertion and removal (e.g., battery, compute unit, sensor pod) without disrupting operational availability or requiring system restart.

FR7.3: Predict imminent component failures using condition monitoring (sensor data, statistical models, anomaly detection) and trigger preventive maintenance or graceful degradation before failure occurs.
FR7.4: Detect, isolate, and diagnose faults in Vehicle, Teleoperation, Network Link, and NGHE Solution subsystems to enable rapid root-cause analysis and corrective action.

FR7.5: Maintain operational availability and local failover capability when Network Link is unavailable or degraded, without dependency on real-time network communication for critical failover decisions.

revise.why: DP7 is logically sound but expressed at too coarse a granularity to guide implementation without risk of monolithic bloat and diffusion. Recommend decomposing DP7 into five child DPs corresponding to FR7.1–FR7.5, each with explicit internal IM (e.g., 'Redundancy Coordinator', 'Hot-Swap Manager', 'Condition Monitoring Service', 'Fault Isolator', 'Local Failover Engine') within NGHE Solution boundary. Additionally, formalize Telemetry and Tasking interface contracts (schema, timing, error handling) and allocate explicit child FRs to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems (e.g., FR7.1.1: 'Vehicle shall report health status every 100ms with <50ms latency', FR7.2.1: 'Teleoperation shall respond to failover commands within 200ms') to clarify subsystem responsibilities and enable independent verification. Define evaluation period, failure modes, and acceptance test procedure for FR7 verification.

assumptions: Predictive condition monitoring can be isolated as a dedicated sub-module or service within NGHE Solution without unacceptable latency overhead.
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can reliably implement health-reporting and failover-response logic as specified by formalized interface contracts.
Network Link failures are transient or can be tolerated by local failover logic; permanent network partition is out of scope or handled by graceful degradation.
Evaluation period for availability measurement (e.g., 30 days, 90 days) is sufficient to capture representative failure modes and validate ≥0.97 target.
Safety Services and NGHE Solution availability logic can be cleanly separated via SafetyAlert interface without implicit coupling.
open_risks: CRITICAL: Predictive condition monitoring may require dedicated computational resources (ML/statistical models) with latency-sensitive requirements. If embedded monolithically in NGHE Solution without clear internal module boundary, monitoring latency could degrade availability performance. Mitigation: Explicit decomposition into FR7.3 with dedicated IM and latency SLA.

HIGH: Telemetry and Tasking interface contracts are not formally specified. Implicit coupling between NGHE Solution and subsystems could lead to fragile availability coordination and verification gaps. Mitigation: Formalize interface specifications (schema, timing, error handling, retry semantics) before detailed design.

HIGH: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems carry distributed health-reporting and failover-response responsibilities. If these responsibilities leak availability orchestration logic, cohesion is violated and subsystem independence is compromised. Mitigation: Interface-driven design with minimal, well-defined subsystem responsibilities.

MEDIUM: Network Link is a critical dependency for redundancy coordination and distributed diagnostics. If Network Link fails, availability architecture may degrade or collapse. Current DP does not explicitly address graceful degradation when Network Link is unavailable. Mitigation: Explicit sub-requirement (FR7.5) for local failover without real-time network communication.

MEDIUM: Verification of FR7 (≥0.97 availability) requires controlled failure injection or field observation over sufficient duration. Evaluation period, failure modes, and acceptance test procedure are not yet defined. Mitigation: Create explicit test plan and acceptance test procedure before gate closure.

MEDIUM: Safety boundary between DP7 (operational availability) and Safety Services (safety assurance) is not explicitly documented. Availability logic could inadvertently influence safety-critical decisions. Mitigation: Document safety boundary and ensure SafetyAlert is the only coupling point.
evidence_needed: Formal interface specifications for Telemetry (schema, timing, error handling) and Tasking (command format, response semantics, timeout behavior).
Latency analysis and resource allocation for Predictive Condition Monitoring sub-module (FR7.3) to confirm feasibility of embedding within NGHE Solution or justify separate service.
Design of local failover logic (FR7.5) that operates without real-time network communication; include state machine, decision rules, and fallback behavior.
Subsystem-level availability FRs and DPs for Vehicle and Teleoperation (e.g., health-reporting latency, failover response time, fault detection coverage).
Test plan and acceptance test procedure for FR7 verification, including evaluation period, failure modes, instrumentation, and acceptance criterion.
Safety case or hazard analysis documenting relationship between DP7 (operational availability) and Safety Services, with clear separation of concerns.
